text: The surgeon isolates the right posterior hepatic artery, secured with a vessel loop. The portal vein dissection follows, using gentle blunt dissection with suction irrigation. The left hepatic artery, surrounded by lymphatic vessels, is skeletonized, double-clipped with Hemlock clips, and divided.

The surgeon navigates small hepatic vessels in the porta hepatis using energy devices. The right anterior hepatic artery is skeletonized, double-clipped, and divided, similar to the technique used on the left hepatic artery. The tissue dissection is challenging due to the proximity of the tumor to the porta hepatis. The portal vein dissection is completed up to the bifurcation, with the left portal vein visible running transversely. The caudate lobe branch is individually clipped and divided, and the left portal vein is circumferentially isolated.

The surgeon uses robotic scissors to sharply divide the double clips on the patient's side. The dissection proceeds to the hepatic duct, focusing on the common bile duct at the bifurcation with the left hepatic duct. The manipulation of the hepatic duct is visibly challenging at this proximal bifurcation.

Following the completion of the portal hepatitis dissection and ligation of the left hepatic duct, portal vein, and arteries, the surgeon performs the Pringle maneuver. The liver's demarcation is confirmed with ultrasound to ensure negative margins. The inflow is controlled, and the surgeon begins the parenchymal dissection.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection and a robotic stapler with a vascular load to clip vessels individually. The stapler is applied without resistance, ensuring safe operation. The metal hepatic vein is visible, and the surgeon performs unpringle maneuvers intermittently to maintain liver perfusion.

The surgeon identifies the right branch of the right hepatic vein in Segment eight, double clips, and divides it along the demarcation line. A small hole at the junction with the inferior vena cava is primarily closed, and another hole in the middle hepatic vein is repaired using proline sutures.

The surgeon completes the parenchymal transection using clips on the patient side and a ventral sealer on the specimen side. The normal liver parenchyma is visible. The specimen is extracted using a 15mm endoscopic bag through the system port. The transection surface of the liver is inspected for any bile leaks, with hemostatic agents available for use.
